<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169676162 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00962: Resource Manager

- page_id: `169676162`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169676162/Resource+Manager
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:20.077+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

7929296

INLINE

7929299

INLINE

319023586

**On this page**

**43**

7929297

INLINE

Resource Manager functionality allows you to manage local resources (installed with a modeling tool or downloaded separately) and the resources available on the server/shared repository.

With the Resource Manager, you can manage different types of resources, such as Profiles, Plugins, Templates, Language resources, Case studies/Samples, Custom diagrams, and others.

The Resource Manager functionality allows you to:

- Install the resources
- Update the resources
- Distribute needed resources internally (including the company's developed resources)
- Check dependency of a resource

The **Resource/Plugin Manager** helps you manage the resources. You can add the resources to your modeling tool from the following locations:

- Local file system/network share
- Webserver

##### Working with the Resource/Plugin Manager

To open the Resource/Plugin Manager dialog

- From the **Help** menu, select **Resource/Plugin Manager**. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Resource/Plugin manager dialog.

To add resources from an *.rdzip* file on a local file system, network share, or a web server

The *core.rdzip* file containing all available resources is added automatically to the **Resource/Plugin Manager** by default.

1. In the Resource Location section, click the [...] button. The Manage Resource Locations dialog opens. Click one of the following:
  1. Add button to add an .rdzip file from a local file system or network share.
  2. Add URL button to add an .rdzip file from a web server. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
2. The file selection/URL dialog opens. Specify the location/URL of the *.rdzip* file and click **Open**.
3. Click OK . The resources of the file are loaded.
4. Select the check box of the resource you want to install.
5. Click the Download/Install button to install the selected resource. [ATTACHMENT filename='Install_a_plugin_through_the_pre-bundled_plugin_file.png']
6. Click Close and restart your modeling tool.

To add resources froma *.zip* file on a local file system or network share

1. Click the Import button. The file selection dialog opens.
2. Specify the location of the *.zip* file and click **Open**. The plugin is extracted and installed automatically.
3. Click **Close** and restart your modeling tool. 
**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

To browse added resource locations

1. In the Resource/Plugin Manager dialog, click on the Resource Location selection.
2. Select the resource location from the drop-down menu. The resources are added to the Resource/Plugin Manager from the added resource file. [ATTACHMENT filename='resource_manager_location_drop-down.png']

To remove a resource location

1. In the Resource Location section, click the [...] button. The Manage Resource Locations dialog opens.
2. Select the resource location you want to remove.
3. Click the Remove button. Click OK . [IMAGE alt='' src='']

****

To uninstall a resource

1. Select the check box of the resource you want to uninstall.
2. Click the Remove button.
3. Click Close and restart the modeling tool. [ATTACHMENT filename='uninstall_a_resource.png']

To see the resource detailsand/or check the dependency of a resource

1. Select the check box of the resource whose details you want to view.
2. Click Details . The dialog expands with the resource description at the bottom. [ATTACHMENT filename='resource_manager_details.png']
3. If the resource has dependencies, they are displayed in the Required compartment. [ATTACHMENT filename='check_the_dependency_of_a_resource.png']

##### Adding resources from a file

The pre-bundled resource distribution file *core*.*rdzip* is bundled with every modeling tool. It is located at < *installation_directory>/data/resourcemanager/distribution* >. The file is automatically added to the **Resource/Plugin Manager**by default.

There are multiple ways to install resources using the **Resource/Plugin Manager**, which depends on your needs and preferences. See the image below.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Visualization of the Resource/Plugin Manager workflow.

To learn more about plugin installation options, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Installing modeling tools and plugins' space='IL2024xR1'] page.

##### Distributing resources using *.rdzip*

Once you have the resource distribution file (*.rdzip*), you can share resources internally, e.g., on a company level.

The resource distribution file (.*rdzip*) contains the resources and their descriptor file. If you are using the web server for resource distribution, make sure that the resource distribution file (.*rdzip*) is extracted into the intended server location.

##### Building your own custom resources file

You can bundle your resources into one *.rdzip* file and then distribute that file on a local file system, network share, or web server.

To build the resources file, you need to install the **Development Tools** plugin. The **Development Tools** plugincan be downloaded individually and then installed using **Resource/Plugin Manager**, or it can be found in the resource distribution file (.*rdzip*).

7929295

INLINE

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Distributing Profiles' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Building a resource distribution file' space='MD2024xR1']

The resources are added to the <strong>Resource/Plugin Manager </strong>from the added resource file.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_manager_location_drop-down.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Resource Manager" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p><span> <span>To remove a resource location</span> </span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the <strong>Resource Location</strong> section, click the [...] button. The <strong>Manage Resource Locations</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>Select the resource location you want to remove.</li><li>Click the <strong>Remove</strong> button. Click <strong>OK</strong>.<br /><strong> <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="remove_a_resource_location.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Resource Manager" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br /></strong></li></ol><p><strong> <br /></strong></p><p><span>To uninstall a resource</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Select the check box of the resource you want to uninstall.</li><li>Click the <strong>Remove</strong> button.</li><li>Click <strong>Close</strong> and restart the modeling tool.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="uninstall_a_resource.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Resource Manager" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><p><br /></p><p>To see the resource details<span><span> and/or check the dependency of a resource</span> </span></p><hr /><ol><li>Select the check box of the resource whose details you want to view.</li><li>Click <strong>Details</strong>. The dialog expands with the resource description at the bottom.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_manager_details.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Resource Manager" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li><li>If the resource has dependencies, they are displayed in the <strong>Required</strong> compartment.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="check_the_dependency_of_a_resource.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Resource Manager" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ol><h3>Adding resources from a file</h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09363c79-db5b-44f3-ab1b-04b7f56ab351"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The pre-bundled resource distribution file <em>core</em>.<em>rdzip</em> is bundled with every modeling tool. It is located at <span>&lt;</span> <em>installation_directory&gt;/data/resourcemanager/distribution</em> <span>&gt;. The file is automatically added to the <strong>Resource/Plugin Manager </strong>by default.</span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>There are multiple ways to install resources using the <strong>Resource/Plugin Manager</strong>, which depends on your needs and preferences. See the image below.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-bottom: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="resource_manager_visualization.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Resource Manager" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> <br />Visualization of the Resource/Plugin Manager workflow.</h6><p>To learn more about plugin installation options, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL2024xR1" ri:content-title="Installing modeling tools and plugins" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Installing plugins]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</p><h3>Distributing resources using <em style="font-size: 16.0px;letter-spacing: -0.006em;">.rdzip </em></h3><p>Once you have the resource distribution file (<em>.rdzip</em>), you can share resources internally, e.g., on a company level. </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b6e1c583-d6c6-4aa4-9584-6692569bb3a2"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The resource distribution file (.<em>rdzip</em>) contains the resources and their descriptor file. If you are using the web server for resource distribution, make sure that the resource distribution file (.<em>rdzip</em>) is extracted into the intended server location.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Building your own custom resources file</h3><p>You can bundle your resources into one <em>.rdzip</em> file and then distribute that file on a local file system, network share, or web server.</p><p>To build the resources file, you need to install the <strong>Development Tools</strong> plugin. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The</span> <strong>Development Tools </strong> <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">plugin </span>can be downloaded individually and then installed using <strong>Resource/Plugin Manager</strong>, or it can be found in the resource distribution file (.<em>rdzip</em>).</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="06f3c676-7b41-4b90-8f00-3a0c0f02e65f"><ac:rich-text-body>For more information about building the resource distribution file, read <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Building a resource distribution file" /></ac:link>.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="974e6b48-d65f-4339-aa9f-f6e4bb8c82be"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">7929295</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Distributing custom diagrams" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Distributing Resources" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Distributing Profiles" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Building a resource distribution file" /></ac:link><br /><br /></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169678179 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00963: Retrieving Slot Values

- page_id: `169678179`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169678179/Retrieving+Slot+Values
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-02-01T16:17:57.879+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template variables
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

126605373

126605376

126605371

**On this page**

32

126605376

Instead of using Stereotype and Tag, some projects may use Instance Specification and Slot to define new attributes of an element.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Instance Specification and Slot defining element attributes.

You can retrieve a slot value by either using a normal UML Specification structure, or by using a slot property.

#### Using normal UML Specification to Retrieve Slot Values

For example, to retrieve a Name slot value type:

```text
0)
				#set($v = $slot.value.get(0).text)
			#else
				#set($v = "")
			#end
			Slot value is $v
		#end
	#end
#end]]>
```

#### Using Slot Property to Retrieve Slot Values

You can use a slot property as a shortcut to get the following information from a slot.

##### Retrieving slot information from an element

The following code retrieves slot information from an element.

```text

```

In which:

- $element is an element.
- slots is a property for getting slot variables.

For example, if you want to obtain a collection of slot variables of an element, type:

```text

```

###### Retrieving slot information from an element and a classifier’s name

The following code retrieves slot information from an element and a classifier’s name.

```text

```

Where:

- $element is an element.
- slots is a property for getting the slots variable.
- ClassifierName is the classifier’s name.

The code returns:

- A list of slot variables whose classifier names are ClassifierName.
- If there are multiple classifiers that match the ClassifierName, the code returns the slot property of the first classifier.
- If there is no classifier that matches the ClassifierName, the code returns null.

For example, if you want to retrieve all slot values from the “Communicate” classifier, type:

```text

```

###### Retrieving slot value from an element, a classifier’s Name, and a defining feature name

```text

```

Where:

- **$element** is an element.
- **slots** is a property for getting slots variable.
- **ClassifierName** is the classifier’s name.
- **DefiningFeatureName** is the name of a slot.

The code returns:

- A slot value whose name is DefiningFeatureName and a classifier whose name is ClassifierName.
- If the slot multiplicity is < =1 (less than or equal to 1), it will return the value in ValueSpecification.
- If the slot multiplicity is > 1, it will return the value in array of ValueSpecification.

For example, if you want to retrieve the values of a slot whose name is “Address” and those of a classifier whose name is “Communicate”, type:

```text

```

- ClassifierName and DefiningFeatureName are case-sensitive properties.
- You can use: $report.getSlotValue($element, ClassifierName, DefiningFeatureName) to retrieve the slot value. See $report below for further details.

##### Code examples to retrieve slot values

This section explains how to get the value of an instance slot from a classifier. The figure below shows the elements of a slot variable in the Containment tree. You can see the values of the element **Member** in the tree, or you can right-click on it and open the Specification dialog to see the values in the dialog.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
An example of elements in the Containment tree.

The following figure shows the Instance Specification dialog of the element Member of the slot variable. As you can see in the dialog, the element Member of the slot variable contains slot names and values.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
All slots of Instance Specification.

The following are examples of using the code to retrieve a value of a slot.

Example 1.

If you want to print the value of a slot **Address** of the element **Member** from the classifier **Communicate,**you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity = 1.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is :

```text

```

Example 2.

If you want to print the value of a slot **Telephone** of the element **Member** from the classifier **Communicate**, you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity is > 1, [1..*].

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 3.

If you want to print the value of a slot Name of the element Member from the classifier Personal Data, you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity = 1.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 4.

If you want to print the value of a slot **Nickname** of the element **Member** from the classifier **Personal Data**, you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity is > 1, [0..*].

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 5.

If you want to use $Report method to print the value of a slot **Name** of the element **Member** from the classifier**Personal Data**, you can use the following template code.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 6.

If you want to use $Report method to print the value of a slot **Nickname** of the element **Member** from the classifier **Personal Data**, you can use the following template code.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 7.

If you want to print the names and values of all classifiers and the slot properties of all instance elements, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.

```text

The output of the code shown above is:

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d06e539-0d58-446f-833f-a51625c663e5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">126605373</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="096acd2b-02ca-46a2-835c-c2a3d730d9bc"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">126605376</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4154f92b-3c0f-4bb0-be68-81013ec6f373"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">126605371</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bf58dd29-bc69-4a3c-bf4d-8ece4bda8e35"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d39e1f93-7c8a-4fa2-a5f2-5690a286f3d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">126605376</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Instead of using Stereotype and Tag, some projects may use Instance Specification and Slot to define new attributes of an element.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ins_spec_slot.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Retrieving Slot Values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />Instance Specification and Slot defining element attributes.</h6><p>You can retrieve a slot value by either using a normal UML Specification structure, or by using a slot property.</p><h2>Using normal UML Specification to Retrieve Slot Values</h2><p>For example, to retrieve a Name slot value type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3b4989e1-d02f-4ff1-b5ed-8b8ad7f9159a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
	#foreach($slot in $instance.slot)
		#if($slot.definingFeature.name == 'Name')
			#if($slot.value.size() > 0)
				#set($v = $slot.value.get(0).text)
			#else
				#set($v = "")
			#end
			Slot value is $v
		#end
	#end
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Using Slot Property to Retrieve Slot Values</h2><p>You can use a slot property as a shortcut to get the following information from a slot.</p><h3>Retrieving slot information from an element</h3><p>The following code retrieves slot information from an element.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b39cf1fe-654a-4b5e-b671-91481733c9d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$element.slots]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In which:</p><ul><li>$element is an element.</li><li>slots is a property for getting slot variables.</li></ul><p>For example, if you want to obtain a collection of slot variables of an element, type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3eac2eb2-b0cc-47f3-9ed1-c8b041d4f4d8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($slot in $instanceSpecification.slots)
	$slot.name
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Retrieving slot information from an element and a classifier’s name</h4><p>The following code retrieves slot information from an element and a classifier’s name.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c455d57f-b3d8-4973-b2be-72be49ccc4b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$element.slots.ClassifierName
or
$element.slots.get("ClassifierName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Where:</p><ul><li>$element is an element.</li><li>slots is a property for getting the slots variable.</li><li>ClassifierName is the classifier’s name.</li></ul><p>The code returns:</p><ul><li>A list of slot variables whose classifier names are ClassifierName.</li><li>If there are multiple classifiers that match the ClassifierName, the code returns the slot property of the first classifier.</li><li>If there is no classifier that matches the ClassifierName, the code returns null.</li></ul><p>For example, if you want to retrieve all slot values from the “Communicate” classifier, type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cb72fff2-3360-41d6-ac20-1765a8cf551c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$instanceSpecification.slots.Communicate
$instanceSpecification.slots.get("Communicate")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h4>Retrieving slot value from an element, a classifier’s Name, and a defining feature name</h4><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5432ff95-f5fb-4799-8564-bcc19ee954c2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$element.slots.ClassifierName.DefiningFeatureName
or

or
$element.slots.get("ClassifierName").DefiningFeatureName
or
$element.slots.get("ClassifierName").get("DefiningFeatureName")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Where:</p><ul><li><em><strong>$element</strong></em> <br />is an element.</li><li><em><strong>slots</strong></em> <br />is a property for getting slots variable.</li><li><em><strong>ClassifierName</strong></em> <br />is the classifier’s name.</li><li><em><strong>DefiningFeatureName</strong></em> <br />is the name of a slot.</li></ul><p>The code returns:</p><ul><li>A slot value whose name is DefiningFeatureName and a classifier whose name is ClassifierName.</li><li>If the slot multiplicity is &lt; =1 (less than or equal to 1), it will return the value in ValueSpecification.</li><li>If the slot multiplicity is &gt; 1, it will return the value in array of ValueSpecification.</li></ul><p>For example, if you want to retrieve the values of a slot whose name is “Address” and those of a classifier whose name is “Communicate”, type:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4bf54286-94b7-4664-a620-cf6d758e3dd8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$instanceSpecification.slots.Communicate.Address
$instanceSpecification.slots.Communicate.get("Address")
$instanceSpecification.slots.get("Communicate").Address
$instanceSpecification.slots.get("Communicate").get("Address")]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e75cb621-b6ac-4177-9752-236ea0dea673"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>ClassifierName and DefiningFeatureName are case-sensitive properties.</li><li><p>You can use:</p><pre>$report.getSlotValue($element, ClassifierName, DefiningFeatureName)</pre><p>to retrieve the slot value. See $report below for further details.</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Code examples to retrieve slot values</h3><p>This section explains how to get the value of an instance slot from a classifier. The figure below shows the elements of a slot variable in the Containment tree. As you can see in the dialog, the element Member of the slot variable contains slot names and values.</p><h6 style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:title="All Slots of Instance Specification" ac:alt="All Slots of Instance Specification"><ri:attachment ri:filename="insspec_slot.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Retrieving Slot Values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br />All slots of Instance Specification.</h6><p>The following are examples of using the code to retrieve a value of a slot.</p><p><br /></p><p>Example 1.</p><p>If you want to print the value of a slot <strong>Address</strong> of the element <strong>Member</strong> from the classifier <strong>Communicate, </strong>you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity = 1.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0b922420-d26b-442a-bda9-f81a968391d4"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  	Slot value is $instance.slots.Communicate.Address
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is :</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3c2639a0-e006-4173-ab7d-371915f632d1"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Slot value is US]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 2.</p><p>If you want to print the value of a slot <strong>Telephone</strong> of the element <strong>Member</strong> from the classifier <strong>Communicate</strong>, you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity is &gt; 1, [1..*].</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="219a764c-2699-401d-a7e2-bdf9865f469c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  	Slot value is $instance.slots.Communicate.Telephone
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ea85fd57-1362-4570-91e9-26604f9f5efc"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Slot value is [0899999999, 0811111111, 02222222]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 3.</p><p>If you want to print the value of a slot Name of the element Member from the classifier Personal Data, you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity = 1.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="835d8231-1391-43a5-9daf-bf5a9a91e8f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  	Slot value is $instance.slots.get("Personal Data").get("Name")
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db1e3d50-e33f-4396-b2fc-4cc92e690571"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[	Slot value is Peter]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Example 4.</p><p>If you want to print the value of a slot <strong>Nickname</strong> of the element <strong>Member</strong> from the classifier <strong>Personal Data</strong>, you can use the following template code as a shortcut. Note that the slot multiplicity is &gt; 1, [0..*].</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="011960e3-c451-4bd2-972a-b0c80d35d260"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  	Slot value is $instance.slots.get("Personal Data").get("Nickname")
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ba67d40e-fdee-48c3-ba22-15479f2c2610"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Slot value is [Peet, P]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 5.</p><p>If you want to use $Report method to print the value of a slot <strong>Name</strong> of the element <strong>Member</strong> from the classifier<strong> Personal Data</strong>, you can use the following template code.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d5202526-2083-47a6-b173-021d5420ae96"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  	Slot value is $report.getSlotValue($instance, "Personal Data", "Name")
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b5170855-ce3d-472d-a861-c14836fe612f"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Slot value is [Peter]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 6.</p><p>If you want to use $Report method to print the value of a slot <strong>Nickname</strong> of the element <strong>Member</strong> from the classifier <strong>Personal Data</strong>, you can use the following template code.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cd460782-44cb-49a4-86ff-66ee419a0224"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  	Slot value is $report.getSlotValue($instance, "Personal Data", "Nickname")
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3217cea0-b93a-4f1b-9c06-ca007a8c966e"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[ Slot value is [Peet, P]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 7.</p><p>If you want to print the names and values of all classifiers and the slot properties of all instance elements, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae0ce5a7-fc3f-4c91-950b-a438ca841ce2"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($instance in $InstanceSpecification)
  #foreach($classifier in $instance.slots)
	Classifier name: $classifier.name
	Slots:
      #foreach($slot in $classifier)
	   $slot.name : $slot.value
      #end
 
  #end
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4225d8a6-12a2-4e96-a28b-386d75d8d4ab"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Classifier name: Personal Data
	Slots:
  	   Name : Peter
  	   Age : 20
  	   Nickname : [Peet, P]
  	   Gender : Male
  	   ID : 1234
 
	Classifier name: Communicate
	Slots:
  	   Telephone : [0899999999, 0811111111, 02222222]
  	   E-mail : [a@example.com, b@example.com]
  	   Address : US
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00964: Retrieving tag values

###### An example of elements of a tag variable in the Containment tree and all tags of a Class.

#### Using normal UML specifications to retrieve tag values

The sample code of usage of normal UML specifications to retrieve tag values is as follows:

```text
0)
        		#set($t = $tag.value.get(0))
        	#else
            	#set($t = "")
        	#end
        	Tag value is $t
        #end
	#end
#end]]>
```

#### Using tag properties to retrieve tag values

You can use tag properties as a shortcut to get the following information from tags.

##### Retrieving tag information from elements

The following code retrieves tag information from an element.

```text

```

Given that:

- $element is an element.
- tags is a property for getting the tag variable.

For example, if you want to obtain a collection of tag properties of an element, type the following line:

```text

```

##### Retrieving tag information from elements and stereotype names

The following code retrieves tag information from an element and a stereotype name.

```text

```

or

```text

```

Given that:

- $element is an element.
- tags is a property for getting the tag variable.
- StereotypeName is the name of a stereotype.

The code returns a list of tag variables whose tag stereotype names are **StereotypeName**.

#### NOTE: Note

Note

- If there are multiple tag stereotype names that match StereotypeName , the code returns the tag property of the first tag stereotype name.
- If there is no tag stereotype name that matches StereotypeName , the code returns null.

For example, if you want to retrieve all tag values from the **Communicate** tag stereotype, type the following:

```text

```

`or`

```text

```

##### Retrieving tag values from elements, tag stereotype names, and tag names

Type one of the following lines:

```text

```

```text

```

```text

```

```text

```

Given that:

- $element is an element.
- tags is a property for getting the tag variable.
- StereotypeName is the name of a stereotype.
- TagName is the name of a tag.

The code returns the following:

- A tag value named TagName and a tag stereotype named StereotypeName .
- If the tag multiplicity is < =1 (less than or equal to 1), it will return a value.
- If the tag multiplicity is > 1, it will return an array of values.

For example, if you want to retrieve the values of a tag named **Address** and those of a tag stereotype named **Communicate**, type the following lines:

```text

```

#### NOTE: Note

Note

**StereotypeName** and **TagName** are case-sensitive properties.

#### Code examples to retrieve tag values

This section explains how to get the value of a tag from a class. The figure below shows the elements of a tag variable in the Containment tree. You can see the values of the **Member** element in the tree, or you can right-click it and select **Specification** to open the **Specification** dialog to see the values.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of elements of a tag variable in the Containment tree.

The following figure shows the **Specification** dialog of the **Member** element of the tag variable. The **Member** element of the tag variable contains tag names and values.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### All tags of a Class.

The following code examples are for retrieving the value of a tag.

Example 1:

If you want to print the **Address** tag value of the **Member** element from the **Communicate stereotype,**you can use the following template code as a shortcut.

Note that the tag multiplicity *=* *1*.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 2:

If you want to print the **Telephone** tag value of the **Member** element from the **Communicate stereotype**, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.

Note that the tag multiplicity is *> 1, [1..*]*.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 3:

If you want to print the **Name** tag value of the **Member** element from the **Personal Data**stereotype, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.

Note that the tag multiplicity *=* *1*.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 4:

If you want to print the **Nickname** tag value of the **Member** element from the **Personal Data stereotype**, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.

Note that the tag multiplicity is *> 1, [0..*]*.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

Example 5:

If you want to print the names and values of all stereotypes and the tag properties of all class elements, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.

```text

```

The output of the code shown above is:

```text

```

<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2d26bfa2-8c6a-428d-81e6-900e0a79980f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">556887862</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f5b88173-958f-4b73-a833-ff158e3bddbf"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">556887866</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c0f684f8-4123-4c24-a9d9-49a7a5b57f5a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">556887858</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>On this page</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="toc" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b48e59ae-c7c8-4a44-88df-4753946893e7"><ac:parameter ac:name="maxLevel">3</ac:parameter><ac:parameter ac:name="minLevel">2</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="71ad8cc2-a627-499c-aec0-f35f887fc2de"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">556887865</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Tag Values.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Retrieving tag values" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">An example of elements of a tag variable in the Containment tree and all tags of a Class.</h6><h2>Using normal UML specifications to retrieve tag values</h2><p>The sample code of usage of normal UML specifications to retrieve tag values is as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1f051079-0cde-4e27-b32b-acee013cb77c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($class in $Class)
	#foreach($tag in $class.taggedValue)
		#if($tag.tagDefinition.name == "Name")
			#if($tag.value.size() > 0)

        	#else

        	#end

#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h2>Using tag properties to retrieve tag values</h2><p>You can use tag properties as a shortcut to get the following information from tags.</p><h3>Retrieving tag information from elements</h3><p>The following code retrieves tag information from an element.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="20a664f6-a7b1-449f-b897-bd9d19316188"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$element.tags]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>Given that:</p><ul><li><strong>$element</strong> is an element.</li><li><strong>tags</strong> is a property for getting the tag variable.</li></ul><p>For example, if you want to obtain a collection of tag properties of an element, type the following line:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="3437bad3-8dd8-4e06-bedb-3dc54d781b16"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($tagProperty in $class.tags)
    $tagProperty.name
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Retrieving tag information from elements and stereotype names</h3><p>The following code retrieves tag information from an element and a stereotype name.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="85987d6e-0b26-48ed-a718-2514a8571703"><ac:parameter     Tag value is $class.tags.Communicate.Address
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="84eed87e-6920-453d-b013-673b42886303"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Tag value is US]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 2:</p><p>If you want to print the <strong>Telephone</strong> tag value of the <strong>Member</strong> element from the <strong>Communicate stereotype</strong>, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.</p><p>Note that the tag multiplicity is <em>&gt; 1, [1..*]</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6e632674-c73e-4b80-aa4c-7787c2b15ebc"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($class in $Class)
    Tag value is $class.tags.Communicate.Telephone
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="557721da-40ab-4d0b-b890-2374e914f29b"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Tag value is [0899999999, 0811111111, 02222222]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 3:</p><p>If you want to print the <strong>Name</strong> tag value of the <strong>Member</strong> element from the <strong>Personal Data </strong>stereotype, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.</p><p>Note that the tag multiplicity <em>=</em> <em>1</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1eed33e1-343b-43bc-81e0-0e29999b3737"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($class in $Class)
    Tag value is $class.tags.get("Personal Data").get("Name")
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4997521f-3e20-4bf0-8df9-d56686eb0dfb"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Tag value is Peter]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 4:</p><p>If you want to print the <strong>Nickname</strong> tag value of the <strong>Member</strong> element from the <strong>Personal Data stereotype</strong>, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.</p><p>Note that the tag multiplicity is <em>&gt; 1, [0..*]</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cd2e623e-a161-4716-8579-656959277b4a"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($class in $Class)
    Tag value is $class.tags.get("Personal Data").get("Nickname")
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="412e8b35-348c-4ba7-9fc9-9daf722145dc"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Tag value is [Peet, P]]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>Example 5:</p><p>If you want to print the names and values of all stereotypes and the tag properties of all class elements, you can use the following template code as a shortcut.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0eca2d30-f2cd-4d24-aff4-2c9fb8ce53f3"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#foreach($class in $Class)
	#foreach($tagProperty in $class.tags)
    	Stereotype name: $tagProperty.name
    	Tags:
      		#foreach($tag in $tagProperty)
       			$tag.name : $tag.value
      		#end
  	#end
#end]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The output of the code shown above is:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36c598e4-9fe1-44f6-aac7-a615af43a442"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[Stereotype name: Communicate
Tags:
	Address : US
	Telephone : [0899999999, 0811111111, 02222222]
	E-mail : [a@example.com, b@example.com]
Stereotype name: Personal Data
Tags:
 	Gender : Male
	ID : 1234
	Age : 20
	Name : Peter
	Nickname : [Peet, P]
]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677575 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00965: Returning to Merge dialog

- page_id: `169677575`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677575/Returning+to+Merge+dialog
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-04-19T12:44:18.411+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud > Model merge in Teamwork Cloud
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can return to the previously performed merge operation if you notice that you have accepted or rejected the changes, e.g., rejected the element creation, by mistake and need to change your merge decisions.

Returning to the **Merge** dialog is only possible if you have not committed the changes to the server or reloaded the server project yet.

Also, you need to discard the changes you have already introduced to the project before you can return to the **Merge** dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To return to the Merge dialog

- Click Return to Merge in the notification window that appears after clicking Finish Merging in the Merged Result panel. [ATTACHMENT filename='model_successfully_merged.png']

Alternatively, you can return to the **Merge** dialog by performing the merge operation again. This works only if the project has not been reloaded or committed to the server yet.

To initiate the merge operation

1. Do one of the following:
  - In the main menu, go to Collaborate > Merge From . [ATTACHMENT filename='collaborate_merge_from.png']

- 
  - In the main menu, go to Tools > Project Merge . [ATTACHMENT filename='tools_project_merge.png']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target">You can return to the previously performed merge operation if you notice that you have accepted or rejected the changes, e.g., rejected the element creation, by mistake and need to change your merge decisions.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e4a65075-8897-4973-b77d-b2caf4cdac41"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Returning to the <strong>Merge</strong> dialog is only possible if you have not committed the changes to the server or reloaded the server project yet.</p><p>Also, you need to discard the changes you have already introduced to the project before you can return to the <strong>Merge</strong> dialog.</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="discard_changes_to_return_to_merge_dialog.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Returning to Merge dialog" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To return to the Merge dialog</p><hr /><ul><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Click </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Return to Merge</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> in the notification window that appears after clicking </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Finish Merging</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> in the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Merged Result</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> panel.</span><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="model_successfully_merged.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Returning to Merge dialog" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ul><p>Alternatively, you can return to the <strong>Merge</strong> dialog by performing the merge operation again. This works only if the project has not been reloaded or committed to the server yet.</p><p>To initiate the merge operation</p><hr /><ol><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>In the main menu, go to<strong> Collaborate</strong> &gt;<strong> Merge From</strong>.<br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="collaborate_merge_from.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Returning to Merge dialog" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li></ol><ul><li style="list-style-type: none;background-image: none;"><ul><li>In the main menu, go to <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Project Merge</strong>. <br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="tools_project_merge.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Returning to Merge dialog" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></li></ul></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169679245 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00966: Reverse Options

- page_id: `169679245`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169679245/Reverse+Options
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:30.498+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering
- labels: ['java-packages', 'round-trip-set', 'importing-packages']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Note

909884868

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Note

909884870

909884867

**Demo**

- Code Reverse

**On this page**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Note

909884869

The reverse is the opposite of code generation. Existing code can be converted to UML models with the help of the modeling tool reverse feature. In order to perform a reverse operation,prepare the sets the same way you did for code generation, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering Sets' space='MD2024xR1'].

- Choose Reverse from the Code engineering sets item shortcut menu.
- Choose Reverse from the selected set shortcut menu.

The UML model for the component can be reversed in the same way. Simply select the component you are interested in from the browser and click Reverse on its shortcut menu. Models can be reversed without creating a set.

To reverse a model without creating a set

1. In the main menu, click **Tools** >**Code Engineering** > **Quick Reverse**and then choose from one of the needed options. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering Sets' space='MD2024xR1']**Round Trip Set** dialog opens. This menu item is unavailable from System Analyst and System Engineer perspectives.
2. Select the files from the Round Trip Set dialog box, Add Files tab.
3. Click OK . The Reverse Options dialog box appears. [ATTACHMENT filename='reverse_options.png']

The following table outlines Dialog descriptions:

| Area | Element name | Function |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Create class fields as | Attributes | fields are represented in a model as . |
| Associations | Class fields are represented in a model as . |  |
| According to rules | or Attribute creation on the reverse is the ability to enter that help decide if an association or attribute must be created on reverse. |  |
| Resolve collection generics | Reverse engineering can create associations when one class has a collection of other classes and uses Java generics (for example, List<String>). If selected, types of collections will be resolved (property type will not be a collection, but a real type).Predefined container types in Java language properties will be appended by all the same containers in this form: ·java.util.List<$$type$$> where $$type$$ replaced by the value of "type" property when the code is generated. |  |
| Reset already created fields | Select this option if you want to keep previously created UML representation (attribute or association) for class fields. |  |
| Model refresh type | Merge Model and Code | The model elements are updated by code. Elements that do not exist in the code will not be removed from the model. |
| Change Model According to Code | The model will be created strictly by code. Entities in the model that do not match entities in the code will be discarded. |  |
| Vizualization | Visualize reserved model | Classes created while reversing can be added to a diagram. |
| Launch Model Visualizer | After reversing, the Model Visualizer dialog box appears. It will assist you in creating a or (Java only) for newly created entities. |  |
| Create new Class Diagram | After reversing, the Create Diagram dialog box appears. Create a new diagram where the created entities will be added. |  |
| Add to active diagram | After reversing, all created entities will be added to the currently opened diagram. |  |
| Analysis.Create dependencies between | Classifiers | Dependencies between classes will be analyzed and created. |
| Packages | Dependencies only between packages will be created. |  |

Method bodies are not parsed on dependency search. Only static information is used.

If you have a code set combined from several files, you may see changes you wish to model without reversing all the code. Only changed files should be reversed. You can do this type of reversing by clicking the **Refresh** button on the set shortcut menu, or by performing model refresh from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering Sets' space='MD2024xR1'].

##### Importing JAVA .JAR packages

Apart from reversing code operation, **Code Engineering Sets** also allows you to import JAVA .JAR files and their Packages using **Java Bytecode** on the **Code Engineering Sets** shortcut menu.

To include (or import) a JAVA .JAR package with Java code generation

1. Create a new UML project or open an existing one.
2. In the Containment tree, right-click Code Engineering Sets . Select New > Java Bytecode . [ATTACHMENT filename='Java Bytecode.png']
3. Name your new Java Bytecode engineering set and click **OK**. The new Java Bytecode engineering set is created under **Code Engineering Sets** in the Containment tree. [ATTACHMENT filename='New Java Bytecode.png']
4. Right-click your newly created Java Bytecode engineering set. Select Edit .
5. The **Round Trip Set** dialog opens. In the Working Directory box, specify the root directory of the .JAR file that you want to import. [ATTACHMENT filename='Round Trip Set Dialog New.png']
6. In the **All files** list, select the .JAR file you want to import. NoteThe file hierarchy of the imported .JAR file in the Containment tree will start from the working directory you set. For example, if you set the working directory as the modeling tool installation directory, the whole file hierarchy that the imported .JAR file is in the installation directory will be also imported into the Containment tree.
7. Select [ATTACHMENT filename='Add Recursively Button.png'] and click OK . The imported .JAR file is now in the Containment tree under the Model Package, File View Package, and the rest of the file hierarchy of the imported .JAR file. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

418489564

**Related Pages**:

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675698 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00967: Reversing Information Flow direction

- page_id: `169675698`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675698/Reversing+Information+Flow+direction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-05-10T13:38:19.638+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Refactoring
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can reverse the direction of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024xR1'] on [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR1'], as shown in the following example. After reversing the direction, the flow directions are updated in all the related diagrams.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The direction of *Coffee* Information Flow is reversed. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.

To reverse Information Flow direction

1. Select the Connector that has the Information Flow set in the Containment tree or on the diagram pane.
2. From its shortcut menu, select Refactor > Reverse Flow Direction . The direction of the Information Flow is changed and updated in all the related diagrams.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Refactoring' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Connector' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>You can reverse the direction of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /></ac:link> on <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Connector" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Connectors]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, as shown in the following example. After reversing the direction, the flow directions are updated in all the related diagrams.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reverse_flow_direction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Reversing Information Flow direction" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The direction of <em>Coffee</em> Information Flow is reversed. The illustration displays concepts from SysML Plugin.</h6><p>To reverse Information Flow direction</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the Connector that has the Information Flow set in the Containment tree or on the diagram pane.</li><li>From its shortcut menu, select <strong>Refactor </strong>&gt; <strong>Reverse Flow Direction</strong>.<br />The direction of the Information Flow is changed and updated in all the related diagrams.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae7fdd9a-3dbd-4e01-84bb-0fecdc173a0f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Refactoring" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with model elements" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Connector" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169675693 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00968: Reversing relationships direction

- page_id: `169675693`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169675693/Reversing+relationships+direction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2016-05-26T08:43:50.689+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Refactoring
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

The direction of the particular relationship can be reversed. You can reverse the direction of the transition, control flow, object flow, and other relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The relation can be reversed if:

- The selected relationship and elements connected by this relationship are editable.
- The source and target elements are compatible, that is, they can be counterchanged.

To reverse a relationship direction

1. Select the relationship in the Model Browser or the relationship’s path on the diagram pane.
2. From the element’s shortcut menu, select Refactor > Reverse Direction . The direction of the relationship changes.

The direction of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024xR1'] will not be changed on the direction reverse action. Additionally, in some cases when the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024xR1'] is not valid anymore, the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Flow' space='MD2024xR1'] will be removed from the project after the direction reverse action.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Refactoring' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='MD2024xR1']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="81b25724-ea67-492b-b507-8ff31eacebe8"><ac:rich-text-body><p>This functionality is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The direction of the particular relationship can be reversed. You can reverse the direction of the transition, control flow, object flow, and other relationships.</p><p><ac:image ac:alt="Example of direction reverse" ac:title="Example of direction reverse"><ri:attachment ri:filename="reverse_direction.png"><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Reversing relationships direction" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p>The relation can be reversed if:</p><ul><li>The selected relationship and elements connected by this relationship are editable.</li><li>The source and target elements are compatible, that is, they can be counterchanged.</li></ul><p> </p><p>To reverse a relationship direction</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the relationship in the Model Browser or the relationship’s path on the diagram pane.</li><li>From the element’s shortcut menu, select <strong>Refactor </strong>&gt; <strong>Reverse Direction</strong>. The direction of the relationship changes.</li></ol><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f2d7cd84-30e7-4e62-a865-5ffb8a643fd3"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The direction of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[information flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> will not be changed on the direction reverse action. Additionally, in some cases when the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[information flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> is not valid anymore, the <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Information Flow" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[information flow]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> will be removed from the project after the direction reverse action.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="da1d48d6-ef32-4ca2-80de-08f61840eddb"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Refactoring" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR1" ri:content-title="Working with model elements" /></ac:link> </li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677304 space=MD2024xR1 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00969: Reviewing conflicting changes

- page_id: `169677304`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677304/Reviewing+conflicting+changes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2019-10-23T11:23:43.970+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud > Using Lock-Free Editing mode
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Example #2

1866845014

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Example #2

1866845026

1866845012

**On this page**

**33**

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Example #2

1866845016

Even though users usually work on different parts of the model, situations when they start editing the same model elements and, as a result, conflicting changes are detected may occur. In such a case, you will be asked to review these conflicts in the**Conflicting Changes** panel and then take certain action, for instance, confirm the update result or discard the local changes.

#### TIP: Example #1

Example #1

*User 1 and User 2 are working on the same model version: #24. The model contains Class A.*

*User 1 deletes Class A from the model and commits the project to the server. The model version #25 is created.*

*User 2 renames Class A to Class B and commits/updates the project to the server. The conflicting changes occur.*

#### TIP: Example #2

Example #2

*User 1 and User 2 are working on the same model version: #24. The model contains Class A.*

*User 1 renames Class A to Class B and commits the project to the server. The model version #25 is created.*

*User 2 renames Class A to Class C and commits the project to the server. The conflicting changes occur.*

##### Conflicting Changes panel

To open the panel

1. In the main menu, click Window .
2. Select Conflicting Changes from the drop-down menu. The Conflicting Changes panel opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='conflicting_changes_panel.png'] The Conflicting Changes panel.

Alternatively, you can open it by clicking the **Review Conflicts**button directly on the warning that appears when conflicting changes are detected.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The Conflicting Changes warning.

Please note that the possible occurrence of conflicting changes is being checked upon the project update or a commit that requires an update.

##### Composition of the Conflicting Changes panel

The **Conflicting Changes** panel consists of three smaller panels that provide different pieces of information to the user:

- Conflicting Local Changes : shows locally modified elements whose changes conflict with the changes already committed to the server.
- Upcoming Changes : shows the upcoming changes that c onflict with the selected local change.
- Preview Update Result : informs about the changes tha t will be applied after the project update.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Conflicting Changes panels that inform users about the changes that conflict in the project.

##### Symbols used in the Conflicting Changes panel

| Icon | Description |
| --- | --- |
|  | Checks the presence of the conflicting changes in the model. |
|  | Selects the previous conflicting change on the list. |
|  | Selects the next conflicting change on the list. |
|  | Expands elements in the Conflicting Local Changes panel. |
|  | Collapses elements in the Conflicting Local changes panel. |
|  | Confirms the update result affecting the selected element. |
|  | Confirms all update results of the conflicting changes at once. |
|  | Filters confirmed/unconfirmed conflicting changes. |
|  | Shows that the conflicting change has been confirmed by the user. |

##### Resolving conflicting changesResolving conflicting changes

**Confirming update results**

Once you have reviewed the conflicting changes, you can confirm the update result that will be appliedafter the project update. It should be emphasized that remote deletions always take precedence over the local changes.

To confirm the update result

1. In the Conflicting Changes panel, do one of the following:
  - select the specific conflicting element and click [ATTACHMENT filename='confirm.png'] in the toolbar at the top.
  - click [ATTACHMENT filename='confirm_all_update_results.png'] in the toolbar to confirm all conflicting changes at once.
  - right-click the conflicting element and select Confirm Update Result in the shortcut menu. The acknowledged change is marked with a green tick [ATTACHMENT filename='green_tick.png'] . [ATTACHMENT filename='confirming_update_result.png']

###### Confirming the conflicting changes via the shortcut menu.

There is another group of actions you can perform to handle the detected conflicting changes:

- Select in Containment Tree : click to select the element in Containment tree.
- Quick Diff : select to inspect the conflicting changes in a compact form.
- Full Diff : select to inspect the conflicting changes in a detailed form.
- Compare Projects : select to compare the modified projects.
- Discard Changes : select to discard the local changes.

Please note that the discarded changes cannot be undone.

Depending on the action you have performed prior to the detection of conflicting changes in your model, you will see either the **Commit** or the **Update** button in the **Conflicting Changes** panel after confirming the conflicting changes in your project.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Update or Commit button in the Conflicting Changes panel.

1875700929

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing element history and inspecting changes' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Change Sets' space='MD2024xR1']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=169677413 space=MD2024xR1 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00970: Reviewing content history

- page_id: `169677413`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR1/pages/169677413/Reviewing+content+history
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-05-08T12:00:26.031+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud > Reviewing model history
- labels: ['scope-history', 'history']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Stopping collecting changes

1348296724

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Stopping collecting changes

1348296726

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Stopping collecting changes

1348296725

You can view changes in the model in a particular scope at the element level; that is, a selected package or a classifier element (a composed element). The ability to see the history of a selected [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Block' space='SYSMLP2024xR1'], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Requirement' space='SYSMLP2024xR1'], or another composed element is a big benefit for system engineers.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### History of element changes in the scope of a *Book*package.

In the history review panel, you can see various details about the changes between selected versions, such as the list of elements that was changed, when changes were made, and who made changes. Different colors describe if a particular model element is added, deleted or modified. You can also export the content history of changes to a report in a *docx* format.

To view the changes of a particular project scope

1. Select a composed element in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space='MD2024xR1'] or diagram pane and right click it.
2. From the shortcut menu, select Content History . The history review panel opens, where you can analyze changes between selected server project versions.
3. To see differences between server project versions:
  - Double click the project version to see differences between the selected version and the earlier one.
  - Select the list of versions and click the [ATTACHMENT filename='display_changes.png'] button.

#### INFO: Stopping collecting changes

Stopping collecting changes

If retrieving the content history takes too much time, you can click the **Stop**button to complete reviewing changes through versions of the selected composed element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To display version properties

1. Open the Content History panel as described above.
2. Select the project version.
3. In the panel's toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='4.png'] button or right-click the selected version and select the Version Properties command.

The button is disabled if either no version or multiple versions are selected.

INLINE

To generate the content history report

1. Open the Content History panel.
2. On the Content History toolbar, click the Generate Report button. [ATTACHMENT filename='generate_report_toolbar_button.png']
3. The Save As dialog opens to specify the location of a report file.

To generate a project Comparison report (Differences report)

1. Open the Content History panel.
2. In the project versions comparison area, click the Generate Report button. [ATTACHMENT filename='changes_between_versions_generate_report.png']
3. The Save As dialog opens to specify the location of a report file.

In a Differences report you can find project scope and element scope:

- Project scope – name, version, the number of elements and diagrams in the compared project versions .
- Element scope – name, path and element type of the particular model element that was chosen as a content history scope .

ThisDifferences report compared to the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing element history and inspecting changes' space='MD2024xR1']Full Diffreport only includes changes from the chosen model element scope and not the entire model. These changes are included inthe sections **Diagram Differences**, **Model Differences**,and**Changes by Type**of the report.

1348296723

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing comparison results' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing model history' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing historical versions of the project' space='MD2024xR1']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing element history and inspecting changes' space='MD2024xR1']

## PAGE 00971: Reviewing element history and inspecting changes

- page_id: `169677388`
- space_key: `MD2024xR1`

#### EXCERPT: Stopping collecting changes

INLINE

##### Displaying element history

To display the history of an element, do one of the following

- Right-click the element or its symbol. From the shortcut menu, select History . [ATTACHMENT filename='image2018-3-16_11-33-0.png']
- Select the element or its symbol and press Ctrl + H.
- Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='MD2024xR1'] of the element and click the History button [ATTACHMENT filename='history_in_spec.png'] .
- If it is a diagram, right-click an empty space on the diagram pane. From the shortcut menu, select History .

#### INFO: Stopping collecting changes

Stopping collecting changes

If retrieving the element history takes too much time, you can click the **Stop**button to complete reviewing changes through different versions of the selected element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The **History** panel of the selected element then opens at the bottom of the client application window. If you select an element in a used project, the corresponding panel title is **Usage History**. The highlighted row indicates current changes that are not yet committed. To refresh the list of versions, click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button in the panel's toolbar or perform the same actions as for opening the panel.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A list of project versions in the History panel.

You can compare any two versions of an element to see the differences between them.

To display element version properties

1. Open the element's History panel as described above.
2. Select the project version.
3. In the panel's toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='4.png'] button or right-click the selected version and select the Version Properties command.

The button is disabled if either no version or multiple versions are selected.

To display the history of an element in a used project

1. Open the element's Usage History panel as described above.
2. Select the project version.
3. In the panel's toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='display_changes.png'] button or right-click the selected version and select the Show History in Used Project command.

##### Comparing element versionsComparing element versions

To compare project versions in the Teamwork Cloud environment, you are required to have a **Read Resources**[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Permissions' space='TWCloud2024xR1'] for every used project in compared versions.

To compare element versions

1. Hold down Ctrl and select the versions you want to compare. You can only compare two versions. Please note that if you select more than two items, only two (the latest and the earliest versions) will be compared. If you want to compare subsequent versions, you only need to select the later one.
2. On the panel's toolbar, click the [ATTACHMENT filename='quick_diff_button.png'] button or right-click the selection and choose either: [IMAGE alt='' src=''] You can also choose the same commands from the toolbar of the **History** panel.
  - quickdiffQuick Diff – to inspect changes of element property values (such as documentation, type, or multiplicity) in a compact form .
  - Full Diff – to inspect changes of diagrams or element property values in a detailed form, as well as look at the properties of new relationships and directly related elements (such as new or modified attributes and operations).

After you select the **Quick Diff** command, a dialog opens that shows changes in element property values (see the following figure). Different colors help identify new, modified, and deleted values. You can analyze exact changes in the textual value of the selected property in the **Text Diff** box. If you want to see the original texts of both versions, click the Compare property values button [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the toolbar of the dialog to open the **Compare property** dialog and then click **Original Texts**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of the Quick Diff dialog of an element (class).

FullDiffAfter you select the **Full Diff** command for an element, the **Difference Viewer** dialog opens, showing changes in element property values, as well as detailed information for directly related elements and new relationships (see the figure below). For more information about using this dialog, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing comparison results' space='MD2024xR1'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of the Difference Viewer dialog of an element.

After you select the **Full Diff** command for a diagram, the **Difference Viewer** dialog opens, showing all changes in the diagram (see the figure below). For more information about using this dialog, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Viewing and analyzing differences of modified diagrams' space='MD2024xR1'].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### An example of the Difference Viewer dialog of a diagram.

Be aware that Quick Diff is not available for displaying diagram changes. Diagram changes (as diagram layout changes) can be displayed only in the **Full Diff**mode.To see these changes you must initiate the comparison of entire project versions. For this, select a couple of project versions in the **History** panel and on the shortcut menu click**Compare Projects**. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing comparison results' space='MD2024xR1'].

##### Generating reports

To generate an element history report

1. Perform element history as described above.
2. On the History toolbar, click the Generate Report button. [ATTACHMENT filename='Element_history.png']
3. The Save As dialog opens to specify the location of the report file.

1358543554
